particle_size_range: 1–500 μm (four fractions: 1–20, 20–212, 212–500, >500 μm for PET; 1–250 μm for aged PE)
key_metrics_output: Polymer identity (PET, PE), mass fraction (μg/mg), particle number concentration, particle size distribution (Feret diameter), recovery rates, spectroscopic accuracy (μ-FTIR 95%, μ-Raman 91%), reproducibility (SR 45.9–129%), blank contamination (91±141 particles)
abstract: An interlaboratory comparison involving 84 analytical laboratories assessed the accuracy of microplastic detection methods using ISO-approved techniques. Two reference materials (PET and PE powders, 10–200 μm nominal range) were embedded in a water-soluble matrix and pressed into tablets for distribution. Five analytical methods were evaluated: pyrolysis GC/MS, thermal extraction desorption GC/MS, μ-FTIR, μ-Raman, and QCL-IRRAS spectroscopies. Parameters assessed included polymer identity, mass fraction, particle number concentration, and particle size distribution. Results showed high agreement between thermo-analytical methods but variable reproducibility across laboratories (SR 45.9–129%). Tablet dissolution was identified as a critical challenge requiring optimization. Findings will inform ISO standardization documents ISO/DIS 16094-2 and ISO/DIS 16094-3 to improve harmonization and comparability of microplastic analysis worldwide.

particle_size_range: evaluation of particles in a smaller size range is addressed: 1−100 μm for PET and 1−250 μm for aged PE films

participants were asked to group particles according to the longest distance (maximum Feret diameter) into following categories: (1) 500−100 μm, (2) 100−50 μm, (3) 50−10 μm, (4) 10−5 μm, and (5) 5−1 μm
additional size class >500 μm to check how efficient the sieving process was
key_metrics_output: Parameters to be assessed during microplastic analysis are specified in ISO 24187:2023, where polymer type identification, particle number concentration, size distribution, and particle mass in a sample

The reproducibility, SR, in thermo-analytical experiments ranged from 62%−117% (for PE) and 45.9%−62% (for PET). In spectroscopical experiments, the SR varied between 121% and 129% (for PE) and 64% and 70% (for PET)
All participants correctly identified both polymer types
Lowest limits for identification, depending on the instrument, lie within 10−20 μm (for μ-FTIR spectroscopy) and around 0.5−5 μm (for μ-Raman spectroscopy)
abstract: The ILC was organized under the prestandardization platform of VAMAS and gathered a large number (84) of analytical laboratories across the globe
Two reference materials (RMs), polyethylene terephthalate (PET) and polyethylene (PE) as powders with rough size ranges between 10 and 200 μm, were used to press tablets for the ILC
The following parameters had to be assessed: polymer identity, mass fraction, particle number concentration, and particle size distribution
Tablet dissolution turned out to be a very challenging step and should be optimized

The obtained results as well as test materials will be transferred into the ISO standardization body ISO/TC 147/SC 2 to the documents ISO/DIS 16094-2 and ISO/DIS 16094-3, helping to create standards and achieve comparable results worldwide
